titleStmt.title: A Survey of the Eight Books of Caius Suetonius Tranquillus, upon the Twelve Cæsars

docTitle.titlePart: A SURVEY OF THE EIGHT BOOKS

div.head: A SURVEY OF THE EIGHT BOOKS OF Caius Suetonius Tranquillus, UPON THE TWELVE CÆSARS.
The FIRST BOOK, Being the LIFE of Divus Julius Cæfar.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: THE privacy of his Condition for some time, after he had receiv'd his Toga Virilis: Certain accidents which befell him when he was a young Man: his first taking up Armes, his progress in the Wars; his accusation of Dolabella, and his retirement to Rhodes; his being taken by Pirates at Sea his action against Mithridates.

His Honours, and Magiſtracies, with his Deportment, and the Accidents which befell him therein his being made first a Tribune of the Souldiers; his Quæſtorſhip in the farther part of Spain; his return to Rome, and the Suspicion he fell under; his Ædileſhip, and the Several paſſages thereof; his Prætorship, and the administration of his Province in Spain; his return to Rome, his Conſulſhip, his Marriage with Calphurnia, his Government in France, his feveral Arts and Stratagems, to confirm and increase his power his Atchievements during the nine years he had the Command of that Province; the death of his Mother, his Daughter, and his Grand-daughter.

His contrivance of New troubles; the advantage he made of the Publick conſternation; his bounty and munificence to the people of Rome, with the artifice he used to ingratiate with Pompey, the Senate, the Citizens of all Orders, the Freed-men and Servants; but especially all Criminals, Prodigals, and persons in Debt, as also all Foreign Princes, and Governours of Provinces the endeavours of M. and Ca. Marcellus to obstruct him, and the ways which Cæfar took to defeat them.

The Civil War which Cæfar commenced, his retirement into Gallia Cifalpina, and his rendezvous at Ravenna; his Declaration and Pretence: his begining of the War, and his paſſage over the Rubicon; the Progress of his Affairs, the Loſſes he sustain'd by his Lieutenants, his five Triumphs, his liberality to the Souldiers and People, the various Spectacles which he exhibited.

The state of the Commonwealth according to Cæfars establishment; he corrects the Calendar, fills up the Senate, divides the Election of Magistrates with the people, and institutes many things de novo in the Republick his Transmarine Colonies; his inventions to Supply the City, which was almost exhausted his diligence, and feverity in the administration of Justice, his Designs for the Repairing and Adornment of the City, and his ways of Defending and Enlarging his Empire.

His Complexion, Habit, Mene, and Manners, as also his Inclinations in Civil and Military affairs; his Stature, his form, his constitution, and curiosity about himself; his habitation and neatness his manner of entertainments, and the discipline of his Family his modesty in some things, and his immodesty in others; his debauching of several honourable Ladies in the City, and several confiderable perſons in the Provinces, efpecially Cleopatra; his abstemiousness, as to Wine, and his indifferency in his Diet; his Extortion in his Foreign Commands, and in his Civil Magiſtracies; his excellence in Rhetorick, and Eloquence, his Caution, and his Courage his Contempt of Religion, his custom in giving Battle, his Horse; his remarkable constancy, and several instances of it: The Souldiers which he most approved, and the Military difcipline he observed, his indulgence and kindness towards them; and their devotion towards him; his comportment upon a Mutiny, his fidelity towards his Clients, his affability towards his Friends; his lenity in point of revenge, his moderation and clemency, both in his Civil Administrations, and Viltories.

Of Cæfars death; the actions and expreſſions for which he was Jupposed to be justly Slain; The publick Odium that was upon him; The number and Counsels of the Conspirators; The Prodigies which preſaged his death, his murder in the Senate, his Will open'd and read, his Funeral: The fury of the people against his Murderers, and their affection to Cæfar: Several Suppoſitions why Cæfar neglected the Religious admonitions, and the advertisements of his Friends That he died according to his own Election, as to the manner of his death: His Age, his Confecration, and the just Judgement upon his Murderers.

div.head: The THIRD BOOK, Being the LIFE of Tiberius Cefar.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: OF the Origin and Ancestors of Tiberius, of the family Claudii, the variety of their Actions, some to the honour, others to the prejudice of the Commonwealth, of the Branch from whence Tiberius was descended, of his Father, of Tiberius whilft he was but a private perſon, the place and time of his Nativity, bis Infancy, Youth, Adolescence after he had afſum'd the Virile Robe, and his Marriages bis Pleadings upon feveral occaſions, his Sundry Expeditions in the Wars, his Retirement to Rhodes, his defire to Return, bis Involuntary continuance at Rhodes, his changing the Habit of the Romans, and the Contempt be lived in whilst he was abroad. His re-admiſſion after eight years absence.

The preſages of his future greatness, bis private life upon his first coming hack, and bis Adoption afterwards. The honours conferr'd upon him, his Tribunitial authority, his Command in the Illyrick Wars, the Honours decreed him upon the Oonclufion of that War. His Expedition into Germany upon the defeat of Varus. His exact Discipline, his Triumph two years after hereturn'd. The Provinces communi-cated betwixt him and Auguſtus, his second design into Illyricum, but he was called back as he was in his Journey, by the Indiſpoſition of Auguſtus; the Authors reasons why Auguſtus left the Empire to Tiberius: Agrippa murder'd, his care of the Funeral and Confecration of Auguſtus: Tiberius poſſeſſes himself immediately of the Imperial Authority, and yet obſtinately refuses the avowing it a long while;

the causes of his hesitation: his his extraordinary Civilities at his first entrance upon the Government: he discourages Flattery, he despises Calumny, his Humanity in Saluting all persons by their Names: The Shadow of Liberty which he introduc'd, and his deference to the Senate: his feverity which grew upon him by degrees, and gave evident tokens he was degenerating to Tyranny: Several instances of his Cruelty: his regulation of the Expences belonging to his Plays: his moderation in the Furniture of his House: his inhibition of daily Salutes and New years-gifts: He Suppreſſes all Foreign Ceremonies, expels Aftrologers, provides with great care for the security of the peace against Robberies and Seditions: be fuppreſſes ſome Hostile Commotions by his Lieutenants: be reduces ſuch Kings as were troublesome, or suspicions, and by what Arts: his Refidence in the City, and thereabouts, for some years after he was poffeffed of the Empire: bis departure from the City, and retirement to Capreæ: his arrival At the City out of Campania: his return into the Said Island, and his laying aside all care of the Commonwealth The Vices of Tiberius diffembled a long time, break out at Capreæ: his propensity to Wine from bis Childhood; his invention for the accommodation of his private Lufts: his Parfimony, bis Extortion, bis Mother, Brother, Sons, their Wives, Children, Friends, and Familiars: his feverity to the Grecian Philosophers belonging to his Court, Indications of the cruelty of his Nature: his feverity in Cafes of Treason, Libels dispersed of him: How detestable his life was, how subject to Terrors, and Affronts. The opinion of Some who thought Tiberius was ſenſible of the Ignominy which should befall him, long before it happen'd.

His ftature and constitution. His contempt of Religion, his addictedness to the study of the Liberal Arts. His Death, his defire to be brought to Rome, his being carried about Campania whilst he was ill. He dyed in a Village belonging to Lucullus. The Prodigies which preceded his Death, the joy of the People thereupon, and his last Will and Testament.

div.head: The FOURTH BOOK, Being the LIFE of Caius Cæfar Caligula.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: OF Germanicus the Father of Caius Cæfar, his Relations, Nature, and Adoption. The commands and actions of Germanicus, his death at Antioch in Syria. His honourable Character: The Univerſal love that was ſhow'd to him whilst he was living. Expreſſions of the Same affection towards him, at, and after his death. The Wife of Germanicus, and the Children be had by her. Of Caius Cæfar before he was Emperour. Of his birth, the variety of Opinions, at what place, and the ſuppoſitions of Gætulicus and Plinius Secundus refell'd. Why he was called Caligula his Youth and Minority, his Reſidence with Tiberius in Capreæ. Tokens of the perverſneſs and cruelty of his Nature. His Wife Junia Claudilla. He attempts Tiberius with poyfon. Of Caius Cæfar Caligula when he was Emperour, his good actions.

The Univerſal joy at his arrival at the Empire, a hundred and fixty thouSand Sacrifices offer'd in the three first Months. The will of Tiberius made Void by the Senate and the People, and the whole power devolved upon Caligula: A fit of sickness which befell him in the beginning of his Reign, and the admirable folicitude of all People for his Recovery. His popularity, and certain performances of his own, in honour to Tiberius, his Mother, Father and Brother. His Confulship, his Munificence to the People, the Senate, and the Equestrian Order. His Plays, and Spectacles. The publick works which he perfected, began, and deſigned.

His Vices. His Arrogance against the Gods, and Men his insolent expreſſions against his Relations: his incest with his own Sisters. His marriages, how unworthily contracted, how wickedly continued, and how barbarously broken. His manner of behaviour towards his Friends. His pride and violence against the Senate, Magistrates, and all Orders of the People. Tokens of his cruelty. The inhumanity of his actions augmented, by the atrocity of his Expreſſions. The same cruelty express'd both at his Feasts, and Plays. His Rancor and Malice against persons of all Ages. His contempt of all Modesty, both in himself and others. His luxury and profufeness, his rapines.

His Military Transactions, his ridiculons expedition into Germany; The care and preparation for bis Triumph. His design of cutting off certain of his own Legions. His cruelty both in word and action at his return. His entrance into the City upon his Birth-day, by way of Ovation. His Statue, Form, Complexion, and Disease. His great Confidence and Security, and (on the other ſide) his most Ridiculous Fear. His Cloaths, Shooes, and Habit. To which of the Liberal Arts he was most vehemently inclined. His Propensity to Arts of another Nature, his too much Favour for Juch Artists as he approved.

Of the Murder of Caius Cæfar Caligula; ſome former Conspiracies against him Discovered. The last Conspiracy managed by Caffius Chera: Presages of bis Death: his Tumultuary Funeral. His Wife Cæſoniæ run through, and his Daughters Brains dash'd out against a Wall. The Conditions of thoſe Times; and Obfervations upon all the Cæfars which were called Caii.

div.head: The FIFTН ВООК, Being the LIFE of Tiberius Claudius Cæfar.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: OF Claudius when he was a private person. The Archievements, Death, and Honours decreed him afterwards. The Nativity and names of Claudius, his Minority, his being despis'd by all People, by reason of his weakness both in body and mind. The Judgment which was given on him by Auguſtus. His life under Tiberius and Caius, and the contempt they had of him at that time. Several Dangers to which he was expos'd. Of his Empire, and Actions therein. The strange accident by which he arrived at the Government, in the fiftieth year of his age. Having establish'd himself, the next thing he consulted, was the ſecurity of the People; his Piety to Auguſtus, Livia, his Relations, M. Anthony, his Brother, Tiberius, and Caius. His Modesty in the Aggrandiſement of himself. The Love and Inclination of all People towards him. Conspiracies against him.

His five Conſulſhips, his Affiduity, and modeation in adminiſtring Justice. His Inconstancy, and the variety of his Determinations. His Censorship executed wish great inequality. His expedition into Britain, his care of the City. His Works, his Liberality in his Doles to the Peeple. The frequency and Magnificence of the Spectacles he exhibited. Certain new Laws which he made, and establish'd in the Commonwealth, ſome about Ceremonies, ſome about Civil manners, Jome about Military affairs, and ſome about the State of all Orders, both at home and abroad.

The more fecret and interieur Conversation of Claudius in his own Family; his Spouses and his Wives, his Children by three of them, and his behaviour towards his Servants. His Form, and Imperfections. The manner of his Entertainments, his Appetite, his Sleep, his Inclination to Women, and Dice. His cruel and sanguinary Nature, his Fear and Distrust, his Anger, bis Forgetfulness, and Incogitancy, both in his Actions and Discourse. His study of History, and his Writings in that way. His Affection to the study of Greek, and some few Testimonies of bis perfection therein. Of the Death of Claudius. His remorse towards his latter end, for having Married Agrippina, and adopted Nero. His Testament writ by himself, and figned by all the Magistrates. He is prevented by Agrippina, and poisoned, his Death, Funeral, Confecration, and certain Remarkable prefages of bis destructions.

div.head: The SIXTH BOOK, Being the LIFE of Nero Claudius Cæfar.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: THE Original and Ancestors of Nero; the Family of the Domitii, the Family of the Ænobarbi; the Great-Grandfather, Grandfather, and Father of Nero. His defcription before he was Emperour; his Birth, with the time and place, the prefages of his infelicity, his Childhood, and what remarkable things happen'd to him therein. His Adoption by Claudius, his Institution by Seneca; he discovers the inhumanity of his Nature, the notable things which he performed, upon his admiſſion into the Forum, when he was a young man. His good actions after his arrival at the Empire he poſſest himself of the Government upon the death of Claudius, his Piety in the Funeral of Claudius; the Honour he exhibited to the memorie of his Father, and his great respect to his Mother. He reinforces Antium, (where he was born) with fresh Colonies, and a new harbour which he made at a vast expence ſome actions and expreffions of his betokening a good Nature. His Plays, and Spectacles, exhibited in ſeveral forts. Tiridates entring into the City, is received with incredible Magnificence. The Temple of Janus shut up his four Confulships; his methods in hearing of Causes, in examining, confulting, giving judgement, amd other parts of publick administration, his inventions about Building; many thing reprehended, corrected, and innovated; his two Expeditions into foreign parts, his Vices ant Disparagements.

His extravagant inclination to Muſick, bis singing publickly in Rome his affection to Horſes, and the driving of Chariots his journey into Achaia, his ignominions oppoſing himself in allt Contests there. Being return'd out of Greece, he enters Naples, his Chariot drawn with white horses. He enters Rome with Triumphal Pomp. Of Nero's Petulancy, his Luft, his Lavishness and Profusion his extravagance in Building his Covetousnes, and Extortion; his ſeveral Parricides, as of Claudius Britannicus, his own Mother, and his own Wives; his Murders of Sundry other persons, either allied to bim by Confanguinity, or Marriage, his cruelty to others. He obſerved no rules, nor moderationin his Executions. He burnt the City, under pretence of his dislike of the Buildings, and narrowness of the Streets. The misfortunes in his Reign, as a great Plague, and a Defeat in Brittain; his patience, and unconcernment in bearing of Reproach; his declension; the defection in France under the Conduct of Vindex.

He is supported by vain and idle hopes from his Astrologers and others; his deportment upon the news of that revolt, when he was at Naples he writes to the Senate, and begins to be alarm'd , he returns to Rome in great Confternation; he is dejected upon intelligence of the revolt of Galba in Spain, and yet continues his Luxury, and ſloth. His designs how vast in some things, how impertinent in others. The ridiculousness, and ſeverity of his preparation for his journey into Gaul; the envy he contracted by an unexpected accident. He is terryfied by his Dreams, his Auspices, and ſeveral other Omens. Understanding the rest of his Armies had deserted him, he meditates his Escape but layirg that by again, be deliberates upon Several other Expedients, At length he leaves the City, and retires privately to a Country house of Phaon one of his Free-men, where, after long trepidation he kills himself to avoid the inſolences impending. His Funeral, his Stature, and inclination, his Habit his Studies, his Popularity, and Emulation of all such as were in any favour with the People. His defire of perpetuating his Name his obstinacy in the contempt of all forts of Religion; his Death, and how it was generally received.

div.head: The SEVENTH BOOK, Being the LIFE of Servius Sulpicius Galba.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: THE Progeny of the Cæfars extinguish'd in Nero which was intimated before by several Preſages. Galba's extraction who fucceeded Nero, The Elogium of his Family, Galba's Father, Wives and Children. His birth, his Pranomen, Nomen, and Cognomen: The Omens of his future Authority. His reviving an old custome about his Servants and Freemen. His studies, his Wives and his particular obfervance for Livia Auguſta. The honourable commands which he exercis'd both at home and abroad. His condition under Claudius; his adminiſtration in Africa; be is presented with the triumphal ornaments and three facerdotal dignities: his recess almost to the middle of Nero's Empire: He is made Governour of Hifpania Tarraconenfis, where he receives hopes of his future Dominion, he Govern'd that Province eight years, but with much inequality. Being folicited by Vindex to revolt, he accepts the Overture. He is faluted Emperour, ſtiles himself the Lieutenant of the Senate and People, and began to act as Soveraign at first the Gods were propitions, but by degrees all his Counſels are disturbed. Nero being kill'd, having deposited the Title of Lieutenant, he affumes the appellation of Cæfar, and begins his journey for Rome; His Entrance into the City ungrateful, by reason of reports which were spread abroad of his great Cruelty and Avarice. His Administration of the Empire; what things he did well, and what otherwise. All perSons difſſatisfied, especially the Souldiers. The Army in the upper Germany, the first that revolts: He thinks to appease them, by his adoption of Pifo; but gives Otho opportunity thereby to perfect his designs: feveral things which portended his ruine. The particulars of his death: his last words: he is deserted by every body: abused by his Enemies when he was dead. His Funeral; a description of his Age, Stature, and Manners, with a relation of the honours decreed to him after his death.

div.head: The LIFE of Marcus Salvius Otho.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: THE Ancestors of Otho, his Father, bis Birth, Youth; his favour with Nero, for whom keeps Poppæa Sabina, who was taken from her husband, but debauches her, upon which account he was fent away Legat into Spain: Having opportunity of Revenge, he joins himself with Galba: He conceives hopes of the Empire, and by his ambition, and bounty, prepares his way to it. Piſo being adopted by Galba, he enters upon bis deſignes, by the help of a sum of Money he had received, and the afſſiſtance of five Halbertiers which belong'd to the Emperours Guards. The Order of the tranfaction to the killing of Galba, and pofSeſſing himself of the Empire. His demeanor at the beginning of his Empire: he is affrighted by Galba's Ghost. The Commencement of the Wars of Vitellius. Otho defires an accommodation : The fidelity of his Pratorian Cohorts, his Expedition unadvis'd and unhappy, he prevails in three Battles, is overcome in a following one, and refolves to make himself away, his reasons for that reſolution. His Death, his Description, and the General Lamentation that was made for him afterwards.

div.head: The ARGUMENT.
div.p: SEveral Opinions about the Original of the Vitellii, of the Emperours Grandfather P. Vitellius, end of his Sons Aulus, Quintus, Publius, and Lucius. The birth, infancy, and youth of Vitellius the Emperour: His condition during the ſoveraignty of Caius, Claudius and Nero: The honourable offices which he enjoyed, but exercis'd with different reputation: His Wives and Children, his being fent Galba's Lieutenant into the lower Germany. His poverty and indigence at that time. His welcome to the Army, which was then dispos'd to new troubles: being received into the Camp, he ingratiates with the Souldiers in ſuch manner, he is faluted Emperour. His actions and expreſſions as he was upon his journey out of Germany to Rome. His entrance into the City The dishonourable commencement of his reign. He is govern'd himself, and orders all things by the directions of inconfiderable perſons especially one of his African Freemen. His great stomach and his fordid evacuation: his cruelty inſtanced in ſeveral executions, and tortures. His Army revolts within eight Months. He attempts to preserve his Empire. He endeavours to accommodate the differences, but in vain, his reputation in Rome, when the Enemy approach'd: he is taken by their forlorn, and ſlain by them with great infolence, and contempt. The Presages of his death.

div.head: The EIGHTH BOOK, Being the LIFE of Titus Flavius Vefpafianus.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: OF the Family of the Flavii; of the Grandfather, and Father of Vefpafian, the honourable ployments which he bore bis Wife, Children, and Concubine, his condition under Claudius, and his Exploits in bis Wars, his imployments under Nero; his Command in the Wars against the Jews: Otho and Vitellius being in contest for the Empire, he conceives hopes of carrying it from them both, upon remembrance of certain old Prodigies part of the Mæſian Army proclaims him Emperour at Aquileia; Tiberius Alexander having the Command of the Forces in Alexandria, declares for him, and afterwards the Army in Judea: His beginnings encouraged by feveral accidents: He comes to Alexandria, where his hopes are confirmed de novo: Receiving the news of the death of Vitellius, he takes the Empire upon him, and makes himself the more venerable by certain Miracles which he perform'd: Vefpafian returns to Rome; he take upon him the Cenforſhip, and establishes the Commonwealth: Having repair'd ſeveral old publick Works, and erected feveral new, he added great beauty to the City: He purges the Senate, and Equestrian Order, and puts better men in their places: He regulates his Laws, restrains Luxury, and Luft: His Civility and Clemency; his great greediness after Money, yet whatever he came by illegally, he diſpos'd of to the publick advantage: His bounty to all people the infamy conceiv'd against him, upon his former Covetousness: His Stature, health, way he preferv'dit: His raillery, and jeering: His disease, death, and the fore-knowledge he had of his own fate, and his Families.

div.head: The LIFE of Titus Flavius Vespasianus Augustus.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: HIS Birth, and Education, the Empire promis'd him by Metoposcopy; the endowments both of his body and mind; bis Military education, his pleading at the Bar in Causes of extraordinary importance; his Wives and Children: Having the Command of a Legion in the Wars against the Jews, he behaves himself with great Courage and Gallantry. Being sent by his Father to congratulate Galba, upon his advancement to the Empire, hearing of the new Commotions, he returns back to him upon his journey, who Conquers Judea, and is faluted Emperour: He is suspected to have run from his Father but he returns to Rome, takes off all fufpicions that were upon him, and behaves himself as a true Protector of the people: He takes upon himself the care of all Offices, particularly the StewardShip of his Fathers Palace, which was never executed before by any above a Roman Knight. He was afpers'd with being Cruel, Luxuriou, Libidinous, and Rapacious but at his coming to the Crown, he diſpell'd those Suspicions easily. His wonderful bounty to all forts of people: The misfortunes and disasters which fell out in his time, he remedied by his benignity: He took upon himself the High Priesthood, that he might keep his hands and his faith pure and immaculate: His death, and compunction for one action only: The time, and place where he died, and the general and inexpreſſible Jadness through the whole City.

div.head: The LIFE of Titus Flavius Domitianus.
The ARGUMENT.
div.p: WHen, and where he was born how he demeaned himself in his youth his Conduct in the Wars of Vitellius; he is faluted by the name of Cæfar, andunder pretence of his Pratorship of the City, exercises Soveraign Authority in the absence of his Father: He undertakes an Expedition into France and Germany, in emulation of his Brother; he pretends to modesty, and the study of Poetry he attends all occafions of making himself absolute; his deportment towards his Brother, after his Fathers death, his conſtant retirement for an hour every day; bis Wife Domitia; how he carried himself in his publick administrations; the magnificent and ſumptuous Spectacles which he exhibited to the peop'e; be restores the Plays exhibited formerly by the Quastors, and is present at them constantly himself; he institutes to Jupiter Capitolinus certain Contentions in wrestling, Musick, Oratory, Poetry, &c. His liberality to the people, the Senate, and the Equestrian Order; the Buildings which he repaired or erelted, his Expeditions partly spontaneous, and partly of neceffity; his Victory over Antonius Primus, known at Rome by certain preſages, before any intelligence could be brought. Many things innovated by him for the common advantage; his method in judicial affairs he restrains the Exorbitances of the City Magistrates, and the Presidents of Provinces: Having affum'd the Censorship, be executes it with great feverity: At first he was gentle and merciful, but afterwards degenerated, and became cruel, and by degrees avaritious, and full of all manner of Extortion: His Barbarity, Confidence, and Arrogance: His Friends, his Servants, and his Wife confpire against him: His anxiety and disquiet upon his fore-knowledge of the year, day, aud hour he should dye: His caution to evade bis destruction, haſtens it: Several Prodigies which preſaged his death: The terrour which was upon him, about the time of bis fatality: The manner of the Confpiracy, and his Murder: The time in which he was flain: his Funeral, his ſtature, the modesty of his Countenance, his Baldness, his Manners and Studies; his libidinous exceſſes his death, how reſented by the Souldiers, Senate, and People. Predictions of future felicity after the death of Domitian.
